raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement functions as a prescriptive directive or guiding principle, instructing a course of action (prayer and meditation) oriented toward a specific relational and volitional goal (conscious contact with a higher power and alignment with its will). It is also confessional and aspirational in register, characteristic of a recovery or spiritual formation context (Alcoholics Anonymous Step 11).",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "A being or force referred to as 'God' exists, or at minimum that the concept is functionally meaningful to the practitioner.",
    "This God has a 'will' that can be known, at least partially, by humans.",
    "Conscious contact with God is possible and can be improved through deliberate practice.",
    "Prayer and meditation are effective means of achieving or deepening such contact.",
    "It is preferable to seek only knowledge of God's will rather than to petition for personal desires.",
    "The practitioner currently lacks sufficient power to carry out God's will and can receive this power through the described practice.",
    "The phrase 'as we understood Him' presupposes that individual or group interpretation of God is legitimate and sufficient for the practice to work.",
    "Aligning one's actions with an external divine will is a net positive for the individual and others."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent on its own terms. The logic flows consistently: improved contact leads to better knowledge of will, and knowledge of will paired with power enables action. The qualifier 'as we understood Him' introduces interpretive flexibility that prevents the statement from being dogmatically rigid, though it also introduces potential vagueness. The restriction to praying 'only for knowledge of His will' is coherent with the broader principle of self-surrender central to the recovery context.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The practitioner holds some working belief in, or openness to, a higher power or meaningful transcendent concept.",
    "The practitioner is struggling with self-will, compulsive behavior, or a lack of direction that external orientation could help address.",
    "Regular contemplative practice (prayer/meditation) has measurable psychological or behavioral benefits for the individual.",
    "The community using the statement provides sufficient interpretive freedom so that diverse theological views are accommodated by 'as we understood Him'.",
    "The practitioner genuinely seeks guidance rather than using the practice to rationalize pre-existing desires as 'God's will'."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "If the statement is used to suppress legitimate personal needs or desires by framing all self-interest as spiritually deficient.",
    "If 'God's will' is defined by an authority figure or group rather than genuinely discerned by the individual, enabling manipulation.",
    "If the statement is presented as a universally applicable truth to people for whom theistic or spiritual frameworks are incompatible or harmful.",
    "If the focus on divine will discourages critical thinking, professional medical or psychological help, or practical problem-solving.",
    "If the phrase 'as we understood Him' is treated as inclusive but functions culturally to marginalize non-theistic, non-Western, or non-binary conceptions of the divine.",
    "If the promise of 'power to carry that out' creates false expectations of spiritual empowerment that, when unmet, induces shame or relapse."
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement functions as a prescriptive directive or guiding principle, instructing a course of action (prayer and meditation) oriented toward a specific relational and volitional goal (conscious contact with a higher power and alignment with its will). It is also confessional and aspirational in register, characteristic of a recovery or spiritual formation context (Alcoholics Anonymous Step 11).
implicit_claims: A being or force referred to as 'God' exists, or at minimum that the concept is functionally meaningful to the practitioner.
This God has a 'will' that can be known, at least partially, by humans.
Conscious contact with God is possible and can be improved through deliberate practice.
Prayer and meditation are effective means of achieving or deepening such contact.
It is preferable to seek only knowledge of God's will rather than to petition for personal desires.
The practitioner currently lacks sufficient power to carry out God's will and can receive this power through the described practice.
The phrase 'as we understood Him' presupposes that individual or group interpretation of God is legitimate and sufficient for the practice to work.
Aligning one's actions with an external divine will is a net positive for the individual and others.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent on its own terms. The logic flows consistently: improved contact leads to better knowledge of will, and knowledge of will paired with power enables action. The qualifier 'as we understood Him' introduces interpretive flexibility that prevents the statement from being dogmatically rigid, though it also introduces potential vagueness. The restriction to praying 'only for knowledge of His will' is coherent with the broader principle of self-surrender central to the recovery context.
conditions_for_usefulness: The practitioner holds some working belief in, or openness to, a higher power or meaningful transcendent concept.
The practitioner is struggling with self-will, compulsive behavior, or a lack of direction that external orientation could help address.
Regular contemplative practice (prayer/meditation) has measurable psychological or behavioral benefits for the individual.
The community using the statement provides sufficient interpretive freedom so that diverse theological views are accommodated by 'as we understood Him'.
The practitioner genuinely seeks guidance rather than using the practice to rationalize pre-existing desires as 'God's will'.
conditions_for_misleading: If the statement is used to suppress legitimate personal needs or desires by framing all self-interest as spiritually deficient.
If 'God's will' is defined by an authority figure or group rather than genuinely discerned by the individual, enabling manipulation.
If the statement is presented as a universally applicable truth to people for whom theistic or spiritual frameworks are incompatible or harmful.
If the focus on divine will discourages critical thinking, professional medical or psychological help, or practical problem-solving.
If the phrase 'as we understood Him' is treated as inclusive but functions culturally to marginalize non-theistic, non-Western, or non-binary conceptions of the divine.
If the promise of 'power to carry that out' creates false expectations of spiritual empowerment that, when unmet, induces shame or relapse.
